We here focus on obtaining efficient and compact CNN architecture, which itself can run on top of compressd videos.
Furthermore, online video understanding works have generated fast processing by reusing computations across frames[60,20].

3. Tiny Video Networks

We use evolution as the main optimization algorithm [10,33] which enables the comstruction of new network structures, can run in parallel, and allows for imposing non-differentiable constraints, such as time and memory limits.

Since the networks need to solve challenging video understanding tasks and at the same time be efficient, the main idea is to create architectures out of basic network building blocks, but allow the selection of effective combinations of layers, working either in the spatial or time domains, which are successful at the video understanding task.

In order to apply evolutionary search for videos, several careful considerations need to be made.

  1. We specifically focus on a set of primitive neural layers, for example, we do not include video-specific elements - such as 3D convolutions, since they are expensice. Instead, elements such as pooling and convolutions are used. The architecture search is tasked with discovering successful sequences of them, which satisfy predefined constrants, e.g. runtime.
  2. The network layers can choose to work in either the spatial or the time dimensions.
  3. The architecture search can decide which input resolution is most advantageous, combined with how many frames per video snippet are needed and at what sampling rate.
  4. We further take advantage of the constraints to eliminate quickly models without evaluating their fitness, if they do satisfy the constraints.
  5. We consider highly efficient network elements: 2D spatial or 1D temporal convolutional layer, 1D pooling, non-local blocks[48], context-gating layers[25], and squeeze-and-excitation layers[14].
  6. In order to limit the search space we organize the architectures in blocks, which can be repeated. Each block can contain one of the available layers. This provides a ceratin structure to the network, rather than exploring an unconstrained sequences of random numbers of layers.

3.1 Tiny Video Architecture Search

entry.title: Two-Stream Convolutional Networks for Action Recognition in Videos
entry.content:
Two-Stream Convolutional Networks for Action Recognition in Videos

2 Two-stream architecture

Each stream is implemented using a deep ConvNet, softmax scores of which are combined by later fusion.
We consider two fusion methods averaging and training a multi-class SVM on stacked L2-normalised softmax scores as features.

entry.title: Understanding the difficulty of training deep feedforward neural networks
entry.content:
Understanding the difficulty of training deep feedforward neural networks

Abstract

Our objective here is to understand better why standard gradient descent from random initalizaiton is doing so poorly with deep neural networks, to better understand these recent relative successes and help design better algorithms in the future.

We first observe the influence of the non-linear activations functions. We find that the logistic sigmoid activation is unsuitd for deep networks with random initalization because of its mean value, which can drive especially the top hidden layer into saturation.
Surprisingly, we find that saturated units can move out of saturation by themselves, albeit slowly, and explaning the plateaus sometimes seen when training neural network.
We find that a new non-linearity that saturates less can often be benefitical.
Finally, we study how activations and gradients vary across layers and during traing, with the idea that training may be more difficult when the singular values of the the Jacobian associated with each layer are far from 1.Based on these considerations, we propose a new initalization scheme that brings substantially faster convergence.

1 Deep Neural Networks

Our analysis is driven by investigative experiments to monitor activations (watching for saturation of hidden units) and gradients, across layers and across layers and across training iterations.

2 Experimental Setting and Datasets

2.1 Online Leaning on an Infinite Dataset: Shapeset-3x2

2.2 MNIST

2.3 Experimental Setting

We optimized feedforward neural networks with one to five hidden layers, with one thousand hidden units per layer, and with a softmax logistic regression for the output. The cost function is the negative log-likelihood.

3 Effect of Activation Fuctions and Saturation During Training

3.1 Experiments with the Sigmoid

The sigmoid non-linearity has been already shown to slow down learning because of its none-zero mean that induces important singular values in the Hessian.

The logistic layer output might initally rely more on its biases (which are learnd very quickly) than on the top hidden activations h derived from the input image.

Eventually bu slowly, the lower layers move toward more useful features and the top hidden layer then moves out of the saturation regime.

3.2 Experiments with the Hyperbolic tangent

3.3 Experiments with the Softsign

4 Studying Gradients and their Propagation

4.1 Effect of the Cost Function

4.2 Gradients at initalization

lots of math formulas, it is really difficult.

5 Error Curves and Conclusions

Serveral conclusions can be drawn from these error curves:

  • The more classical neural networks with sigmoid or hyperbolic tangent units and standard initalization fare rather poorly, converging more slowly and apparently towards ultimately poorer local minima.

  • The softsign networks seem to be more rebust to the initalization procedure than tanh networks, presumably because of their gentler non-linearity

  • For tanh networks, the proporsd normalized inintalization can be quit helpful, presumbly because the layer-to-layer transformations maintain magnitudes activations (flowing upward) and gradients(flowing backward).

The other conclusions from this study are the following:

  • Monitoring activations and gradients across layers and training iterations is power investigative tool for understanding training diffculites in deep nets.

  • Sigmoid activations (not symmetric around 0) should be avoid when initalizing from small random weights, because they yield poor learning dynamics, with inital saturation of the top hidden layer.

  • Keep the layer-to-layer transformatons such that both activations and gradients flow well appears helpful, and allows to eliminate a good part of the discrepancy between purely supervised deep networks and ones pre-trained with unsupervised learning.

  • Many of our observations remain unexplained, suggesting further investgations to better understand gradients and training dynamics in deep architectures.

The saturation is related with activation function and initalization. A good initalization can help network convergence fast.

10

重载、覆盖、隐藏
成员函数重载

  1. 相同的范围(在同一个类中)
  2. 函数名字相同
  3. 参数不同
  4. virtual 关键字可有可无

覆盖指派生类函数覆盖基类函数

  1. 不同范围(分别位于派生类与基类)
  2. 函数名字相同
  3. 参数相同
  4. 基函数必须有virtual关键字

隐藏

  1. 如果派生类的函数与基类的函数同名，但是参数不同。此时，不论有无virtual关键字，基类的函数都将被隐藏
  2. 如果派生类的函数与基类的函数同名，并且参数也相同，但是基类函数没有virtual关键字。此时，基类的函数被隐藏

6

派生类继承了基类的私有成员，但是不能直接访问，只能通过派生类的友元函数访问。

5

虚函数不能使用static

程序段 作用
bss(block started by symbol) 通常是指用来存放程序中未初始化的全局变量的一块内存区域
data(data segment) 通常是指用来存放程序中已初始化的全局变量的一块内存区域
text(code segment/text segment) 通常是指用来存放程序执行代码的一块内存区域
heap 堆是用于存放进程运行中被动态分配的内存段，大小并不固定，可以动态扩张
stack 用户存放程序临时创建的局部变量, 函数调用时，参数也会被压入调用栈中

3

空类中编译器插入一个char类型，用来标识这个class不同实体在内存中配置独一无二地址，故空类大小1字节。

2

C++语言将struct当成类来处理的，所以C++的struct可以包含C++类的所有东西，例如构造函数，析构函数，友元等，C++的struct和C++类唯一不同就是struct成员默认的是public， C++默认private。而C语言struct不是类，不可以有函数，也不能使用类的特征例如public等关键字 ，也不可以有static关键字。

1

const修饰类的成员函数，则该成员函数不能修改类中任何非const成员函数。一般写在函数的最后来修饰。

命令 查看方式
cat 由第一行开始显示文件所有内容
tac 从最后一行开始显示文件所有内容
more 一页一页的显示文件内容，只能向后翻页
less 也是一页一页的显示文件内容，但是可以通过键盘上的PageDown、PageUp控制向后向前翻页，可以光标移动
head 显示一个文件的前几行
tail 显示一个文件的后几行

5

硬链接&软连接2.0
什么是连接?
链接操作实际上是给系统中已有的某个文件指定另外一个可以用于访问它的名称。对于这个新的文件名，我们可以为之指定不同的访问权限，可以控制对信息的共享和安全的问题。如果连接指向目录，用户就可以利用该链接直接进入链接的目录而不用打一大堆的路径名。而且即使我们删除这个链接也不会破坏原来的目录。
硬连接
硬链接只能引用同一文件系统中的文体。它引用的是文件在文件系统中的物理索引(inode)。当您移动或删除原始文件时，硬链接不会被破坏，因为它所用引用的是文件的物理数据而不是文件结构中的位置。硬链接的文件不需要用用户有访问原始文件的权限，也不会显示原始文件的位置，这样有助于文件的安全。如果您删除的文件有相应的硬链接，文件依然会被保留，直到所有对它的引用都被删除。
软连接
软连接，其实就是新建一个文件，这个文件就是专门用来指向别的文件(和windows下的快捷方式的那个文件有很近的意思)。软连接产生的是一个新文件，但这个文件的作用就是专门指向某个文件的，删除了这个软连接文件，那就等于不需要这个链接，和原来的存在实体原文件没有任何关系，但删除原来的文件，则相应的软连接不可用(cat那个软连接文件，则提示“没有该文件或目录”)。

entry.title: Aggregating Local Image Descriptors into Compact Codes
entry.content:
Aggregating Local Image Descriptors into Compact Codes

Abstract

This paper addresses the problem of large-scale image search.
We first present and evalute different ways of agregating local image descripers into a vector and show that the Fisher kernel achieves better performance than the reference bag-of-visual words approach for any given vector dimension.
We then jointly optimize dimensionality reduction and indexing in order to obtain a precise vector comparision as well as preserving high accuracy.

1. Introduction

Image search consisists here of finding in a large set of images the ones that represent the same object or location possibly viewed from different viewpoints and in the presence of occlusions and clutter.

Typical applications include finding locations and particular objects, as well as detecting partial image duplicates on the web and deformed copies.

The bag-of-words representation is the most widely adopted method for this purpose.
There are three reasons for the success of the BOW representation.
First, this representation benefits from powerful local descriptors, such as the SIFT descriptor[7] or more recent ones[8],[9],[10].
Second, the BOW vector representation can be compared with standard distance.
Third, the BOW vector can be high dimensional, in which case the vector are sparse and inverted lists can be used to implement efficient search.
However, two factors limit the number of images that can be indexed in practice: the search time itself, which becomes prohibitive when considering more than 10 million images, and the memory useage per image.

In this paper, we address the problem of searching for the most similar images to given query in a very large image database (a hundred million images).

Most similar to our work is the approach of jegou et al., which proposes an approximate nearest neighbor search for BOW features. However, an image needs to be encoded by more than a hundred bytes to approximate the search accuracy of the inital representaion.

In contrast, our approach obtains a higher accuracy with a very compact representation. This performance is achieved by optimizing:

  1. the representaion, how to aggregate local image descriptors into a vector representation,
  2. the dimensionality reduction of these vector,
  3. the indexing algorithm.

A low-dimesional vector can be indexed efficiently, but its discriminative power may be too low.

Our first contirbution is an image representaion of moderate dimensionality that provides an excellent search accuracy.
Our representation is based on Fisher kernels[20], a power tool to transform an incoming variable-size set of independent samples into a fixed-size vector representation.
We assume that the samples are distributed according to a parametric generative model, in this case a Gaussian Mixture Model estimated on a trainin set.
Experimental results demonstrate that this choice significantly outperforms BOW for the same size on moset data set.
Moreover, it is cheaper to compute because fewer visual words are required. We also evaluate a simplified versionof Fishers kernels, the Vector of Local Aggregated Descriptors (VLAD).

The description vectors are compared using the L2 distance.
Their dismensionality is reduced to a few hundred components by principal component analysis (PAC).

The accoracy is measured by mAP.

3 IMAGE VECTOR REPRESENTATION

3.1 Bag-of-Features

3.3 Fish Vector

3.3 VLAD: Nonprobabilistic Fisher Kernel

As for the BOW, a codebook is first learned using k-means. Each local descriptor is associated with its nearest visual in the codebook. For each codeword , the diffecences of the vector assigned to are accumulated:

Algorithm 1 Computation of the VLAD descriptor V from a set of descriptors . The set of controids is learned on a training set using k-means.

  1. get the codebook by k-means u
  2. get the nearest visual using local descriptor x
  3. sum the difference of u and x
  4. l2 normalization

entry.title: Measuring Saturation in Neural Networks
entry.content:
Measuring Saturation in Neural Networks

Abstract

In the network context, the phenomenon of saturation refers to the state in which a neuron predominantly outputs values close to the asymptotic ends of the bounded activation function. Saturation damages both inforamtion capacity and learning ability of neural network characteristic that can be used to understand the behaviour of the network itself, as well as the learning algorithm employed.

1 INTRODUCTION

Saturation occurs when the hidden units of a neural network predominantly output values closs to the asymptotic ends of the activation function range. Saturation reduces the NN to a binary state, thus limiting the overall information capacity of NN. Saturated units make gradient descent learning slow and inefficient due to small derivative values near asymptotes.

Saturation is phenomenon in which network learn slowly becasue of the small derivative values near the activation function asymoptotes.

What is actually a bit confusing, is that in the docs they state the following about the pooler output: “This output is usually not a good summary of the semantic content of the input, you’re often better with averaging or pooling the sequence of hidden-states for the whole input sequence

258

The difference and use of output and hidden state of an RNN?

The output is the concatenation of the hidden state from every time step, whereas hidden is simply the final hidden state

Generally in an encoder-decoder setting the decoder is initialised using the final hidden state of the encoder. As you say, the idea is that the hidden state should represent the whole sentence.

In a labelling setting the output is generally used. For instance, when labelling parts of speech, for each word input, a part of speech is output. That said, when you are classifying the entire sequence, you don’t want a label for each step of input, you only want a single label at the end, so it seems logical to take the last output.

You could use the last hidden state to produce the label, and it might work marvellously, but then again, if the model uses the hidden state to store its intermediate representations of the sentence, then forcing the last hidden state to correspond to the label might interfere with the use of the hidden state for the intermediate representations.

An interesting experiment would be to take the hidden state and feed it into a linear layer or two in order to predict the sentence label.

There are two existing strategies for applying pre-trained langeuage representations to downstream tasks: feature-based and fine-tuning.

  • The feature-based approach uses task-specific architecture that include the per-trained representations as additional features.
  • The fine-tuning approach intorduces minimal task-specific parameters, and is trained on the downstream tasks by simply fine-tuning all pre-trained parameters.

The vector ([0.4 0.4 0.2]) is the attention 早 on others.
The vector ([1.4,1.4,2,1.8,1]) is the new repersentaton of 早
ref

222

Proof of Positional Embedding

Let $M$ be a $2\times2$ matrix, we want to find $v_{1},v_{2},u_{1}, u_{2}$ so that:
$
\begin{bmatrix}
u1 & v1\
u2 & v2
\end{bmatrix}

\cdot

\begin{bmatrix}
sin(w_{k}.t)\
cos(w_{k}.t)
\end{bmatrix}

=

\begin{bmatrix}
sin(w_{k}.(t+\phi)\
cos(w_{k}.(t+\phi)
\end{bmatrix}
$

By applying the addition theorem, we can expand the right hand side as follows:
$
\begin{bmatrix}
u1 & v1\
u2 & v2
\end{bmatrix}

\cdot

\begin{bmatrix}
sin(w_{k}.t)\
cos(w_{k}.t)
\end{bmatrix}

=

\begin{bmatrix}
sin(w_{k}.t)cos(w_{k}.\phi)+cos(w_{k}.t)sin(w_{k}.\phi) \
cos(w_{k}.t)cos(w_{k}.\phi)-sin(w_{k}.t)sin(w_{k}.\phi)
\end{bmatrix}
$

Which result in the following two equations:
$
u_{1}sin(w_{k}.t)+v_{1}cos(w_{k}.t) = \quad cos(w_{k}.\phi)sin(w_{k}.t)+sin(w_{k}.\phi)cos(w_{k}.t)
\
u_{2}sin(w_{k}.t)+v_{2}cos(w_{k}.t) = - sin(w_{k}.\phi)sin(w_{k}.t)+cos(w_{k}.\phi)cos(w_{k}.t)
$
By solving above equations, we get:
$
u_{1} = \quad cos(w_{k}.\phi) \quad v_{1}=sin(w_{k}.\phi) \
u_{2} = - sin(w_{k}.\phi) \quad v_{1}=cos(w_{k}.\phi) \
$
So the final transformation matrix M is:
$
M_{\phi,k} =
\begin{bmatrix}
cos(w_{k}.\phi) & sin(w_{k}.\phi) \
-sin(w_{k}.\phi) & cos(w_{k}.\phi)
\end{bmatrix}
$

As you can see, the final transformation does not depend on t. Note that one can find the matrix M very similar to the rotation matrix.

221

Positional Embedding
the following criteria should be satisfied:

  • It should output a unique encoding for each time-step (word’s position in a sentence)
  • Distance between any two time-steps should be consistent across sentences with different lengths.
  • Our model should generalize to longer sentences without any efforts. Its values should be bounded.
  • It must be deterministic

220

encoder output produces the Key and Value matries and the decoder of thee Maskedd-head attention block produces Query matrix.

219

einsum = Free indices and Summation index
rules:

  1. repeating letters in different inputs means input those values will be multiplied and those products will be the output.

  2. Omitting a letter means that axis will be summed.

  3. we can permute the order of axis.

218

Transformer decoder: what is different?
The decoder consists of all the aforementioned components plus two novel ones. As before:

  1. The output sequence is fed in its entirety and word embeddings are computed
  2. Positional encoding are again applied
  3. And the vectors are passed to the first Decoder block

Each decoder block includes:

  1. A Masked multi-head self-attention layer
  2. A normalization layer followed by a residual connection
  3. A new multi-head attention layer (known as Encoder-Decoder attention)
  4. A second normalization layer and a residual connection
  5. A linear layer and a third residual connection

The decoder block appears again 6 times. The final output is transformed through a final linear layer and the output probabilities are calculated with the standard softmax function.

217

Sum up: the Transformer encoder
To process a sentence we need these 3 steps:

  1. Word embeddings of the input sentence are computed simultaneously.
  2. Positional encodings are then applied to each embedding resulting in word vectors that also include positional information.
  3. The word vectors are passed to the first encoder block.

Each block consists of the following layers in the same order:

  1. A multi-head self-attention layer to find correlations between each word
  2. A normalization layer
  3. A residual connection around the previous two sublayers
  4. A linear layer
  5. A second normalization layer
  6. A second residual connection

Note that the above block can be replicated several times to form the Encoder. In the original paper, the encoder composed of 6 identical blocks.

216

Multi-head attention allows the model to jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions. With a single attention head, averaging inhibits this.

Key-value-query concepts come from information retrieval systems.
When you search(query) for a particular video, the search engine will map your query againt a set of key(video title, description, etc.) associated with possible stored videos. Then the algorithm will present you the best-matched videos(values). This is the foundation of content/feature-based lookup.

We use the keys to define the attention weights to look at the data and the values as the information that we will actually get.

  • Value: $v_{i} = W_{V}x_{i}$
  • Output: $c_{j} = V \times Softmax(K^{T}q_{j})$

GRU
GRU consists of update gate and reset gate.
The update gate decides how much the unit updates its activation, or content.
The reset gate effectively makes the unit act as if it is reading the first symbol of an input sequence, allowing it to forget the previously computed state.
$r_{t}=\sigma(W_{r} \cdot [h_{t-1},x]+b_{r})$
$z_{t}=\sigma(W_{z} \cdot [h_{t-1},x]+b_{z})$
$\tilde{h_{t}}=tanh(W_{h} \cdot [r_{t}\cdot h_{t-1},x_{t}]+b_{h})$
$h_{t}=(1-z_{t})\cdot h_{t-1} + z_{t}\cdot \tilde{h_{t}}$

  1. batch gradient descent BGD

    梯度的移动指数加权平均
    $v = 0.8v + 0.2\nabla w$
    $w = w - \alpha v$
    对网络梯度处理更平滑，让梯度摆动幅度更小
  5. AdaGrad
    $gradent = history_gradent + (\nabla w)^{2}$
    $w = w-\frac{\alpha}{\sqrt{gradent}+\delta} \nabla w$
    学习率自动更新从而达到自适应的学习效果
  6. RMSProp
    $gradent = 0.8 \cdot history_gradent + 0.2 \cdot (\nabla w)^{2}$
    $w = w-\frac{\alpha}{\sqrt{gradent}+\delta} \nabla w$
  7. Adaptive Moment Estimation Adam
    初始化动量和平方累计量
    $v_{w}=0,s_{w}=0$
    $v_{w} = 0.8v + 0.2\nabla w$
    $s_{w} = 0.8s + 0.2\cdot (\nabla w)^{2}$
    $w = w-\frac{\alpha}{\sqrt{s_{w}}+\delta} v_{w}$
    学习率自适应，梯度振幅越来越小

字符 描述
(?:pattern) 匹配pattern但不获取匹配的子字符串（shy groups），也就是说这是一个非获取匹配，不存储匹配的子字符串用于向后引用。这在使用或字符“(|)”来组合一个模式的各个部分是很有用。例如“industr(?:y|ies)”就是一个比“industry|industries”更简略的表达式。
(?=pattern) 正向肯定预查（look ahead positive assert），在任何匹配pattern的字符串开始处匹配查找字符串。这是一个非获取匹配，也就是说，该匹配不需要获取供以后使用。例如，“Windows(?=95|98|NT|2000)”能匹配“Windows2000”中的“Windows”，但不能匹配“Windows3.1”中的“Windows”。预查不消耗字符，也就是说，在一个匹配发生后，在最后一次匹配之后立即开始下一次匹配的搜索，而不是从包含预查的字符之后开始。
(?!pattern) 正向否定预查（negative assert），在任何不匹配pattern的字符串开始处匹配查找字符串。这是一个非获取匹配，也就是说，该匹配不需要获取供以后使用。例如“Windows(?!95|98|NT|2000)”能匹配“Windows3.1”中的“Windows”，但不能匹配“Windows2000”中的“Windows”。预查不消耗字符，也就是说，在一个匹配发生后，在最后一次匹配之后立即开始下一次匹配的搜索，而不是从包含预查的字符之后开始
(?<=pattern) 反向（look behind）肯定预查，与正向肯定预查类似，只是方向相反。例如，“(?<=95|98|NT|2000)Windows”能匹配“2000Windows”中的“Windows”，但不能匹配“3.1Windows”中的“Windows”。
(?<!pattern) 反向否定预查，与正向否定预查类似，只是方向相反。例如“(?<!95|98|NT|2000)Windows”能匹配“3.1Windows”中的“Windows”，但不能匹配“2000Windows”中的“Windows”。

171

negative sampling
word2vec Explained: Deriving Mikolov et al.’s Negative-Sampling Word-Embedding Method

The idea of word2vec is to maximise the similarity (dot product) between the vectors for words which appear close togeter(in the context of each other) in text, and minimise the similarity of words that do not.
$\frac{v_{c} \cdot v_{w}}{\sum(v_{c1} \cdot v_{w})}$
The numberator is basically the similarity between words c (the context) and w (the target) word, the denominator computes the similarity of all other contexts c1 and the target word w. Maximising this ratio ensures words that appear closer togeter in text have more similar vectors than words that do not. However, computing this can be very slow, beacuse there are many contexts c1. Negative sampling is one of the ways of addressing this problem - just select a couple of contexts c1 at random. The end result is that if cat appears in the context of food, then the vector of food is more similar to the vector of cat(as measures by their dot product) than the vectors of several other randomly chosen words(e.g. democrary, greed, Freddy), instead of all other words in language. This makes word2vec much faster to train.

170

自然语言处理领域中，判断两个单词是不是一对上下文词（context）与目标词（target），如果是一对，则是正样本，如果不是一对，则是负样本。
采样得到一个上下文词和一个目标词，生成一个正样本（positive example），生成一个负样本（negative example），则是用与正样本相同的上下文词，再在字典中随机选择一个单词，这就是负采样（negative sampling）。

比如给定一句话“这是去上学的班车”，则对这句话进行正采样，得到上下文“上”和目标词“学”，则这两个字就是正样本。
负样本的采样需要选定同样的“上”，然后在训练的字典中任意取另一个字，“梦”、“目”，这一对就构成负样本。
训练需要正样本和负样本同时存在。

169

PMI is a measure of association between a feature (in your case a word) and a class (category), not between a document (tweet) and a category. The formula is available on Wikipedia:
$pmi(x,y) = log\frac{P(x,y)}{P(x)P(y)}$
In that formula, X is the random variable that models the occurrence of a word, and Y models the occurrence of a class. For a given word x and a given class y, you can use PMI to decide if a feature is informative or not, and you can do feature selection on that basis. Having less features often improves the performance of your classification algorithm and speeds it up considerably. The classification step, however, is separate- PMI only helps you select better features to feed into your learning algorithm.

Edit: One thing I didn’t mention in the original post is that PMI is sensitive to word frequencies. Let’s rewrite the formula as
$pmi(x,y) = log \frac{P(x,y)}{P(x)P(y)} = log\frac{P(x|y)}{P(x)}$
When x and y are perfectly correlated, P(x|y) = P(y|x) = 1, so pmi(x,y) = 1/P(x). Less frequent x-es (words) will have a higher PMI score than frequent x-es, even if both are perfectly correlated with y.

= P(y_[1]) \cdot P(y_{2}|y_{1}) \cdot P(y_{3}|y_{1},y_{2}) … P(y_{n}|y_{1},…,y_{n-1})

61

N-grams models
$
P(mat |\ cat\ on \ a) = \frac{N(cat\ on\ a\ mat)}{N(cat \ on \ a )}
$
Avoid zeros in the denominator
A more clever solution it to mix all probabilities:
unigram, bigram, trigram, etc. For this, we need scalar positive weights $\lambda_{0},\lambda_{1},…,\lambda_{n-1}$ such that $\sum_{i}\lambda_{i}=1$
$
\widehat{P}(mat | cat\ on\ a) \approx \lambda_{3} P(mat|cat\ on\ a) +
\lambda_{2} P(mat|on\ a) +
\lambda_{1} P(mat | a) +
\lambda_{0} P(mat)
$
Avoid zeros in the numerator
The simplest way to avoid this is just to pretend we saw all n-grams at least on time: just add 1 to all counts! Alternatively, instead of 1, you can add a small $\delta$
$
\widehat{P}(mat| cat\ on\ a) = \frac{\delta+N(cat\ on\ a\ mat)} {\delta \cdot |V|+N(cat\ on\ a)}
$

60

Markov property
The probability of a word only depends on fixed number of previous words.
n-gram
The probability of a word only depends on previous n-words.
Smoothing: Redistribute Probability Mass
$P(mat | I\ saw \ a\ cat\ on\ a) = P(mat | cat\ on\ a) = \frac{N(cat\ on\ a\ mat)}{N(cat\ on\ a)}$

59

N-gram and neural language models differ only in a way they compute the conditional probabilities.
Similar to count-based methods we saw earlier in the Word Embeddings lecture, n-gram language models also count global statistics from a text corpus.
Differently from n-gram models that define formulas based on global corpus statistics, neural models teach a network to predict these probabilities.

BOE is the sum of embeddings and BOW is the sum of one-hot vectors;

57

SVMs with linear kernel perform better than Naive Bayes.

56

Advantages and drawbacks of Naive Bayes and Logistic Regression

  • simplicity
    Both methods are simple; Naive Bayes is the simplest one.
  • interpretability
    Both methods are interpretable: you can look at the features which influenced the predictions most(in Naive Bayes - usually words, in logistic regression - whatever you defined).
  • training speed
    Naive Bayes is fast to train - it requires only one pass through the training data to evaluate the counts. For logistic regression, this is not the case: you have to go over the data many times until the gradient ascent converges.
  • independence assumptions
    Naive Bayes is too “naive” - it assumed that features (words) are conditionally independent given class. Logistic regression does not make this assumption - we can hope it is better.
  • text representation: manual
    Both methods use manually defined feature representation (in Naive Bayes, BOW is standard choice, but you still choose this yourself). While manually defined features are good for interpretability, they may be no so good for performance - you are likely to miss something which can be useful for the task.

54

Laplace smoothing (additive smoothing)
Laplace smoothing is a smoothing technique that helps tackle the problem of zero
probability in the Naive Bayes machine learning algorithm.
$P(x_{i}|y=k) = \frac{ \delta + N(x_{i}|y=k)}{ \sum_{t=1}^{V}(\delta+N(x_{t},y=k))} = \frac{\delta + N(x_{i}|y=k)}{\delta \cdot |V| + \sum_{t=1}^{|V|}N(x_{t},y=k)}$
The $\delta$ is to prevent the zero probability.

53

The Naive Bayes assumptions are

  • Bag of Words assumption: word order does not matter,
  • Conditional Independence assumption: features(words) are independent given the class.

52

the most common classifier is legistic regression(other variants such as Navie Bayes or SVM)

51

Word embeddings reflect context they saw.

neightbors usage/meaning
different changed
the same did not changed

50

subwords are useful(FastText)

  • better understanding of morphology
    By assigning a distinct vector to each word, we ignore morphology. Giving information about subwords can let the model know that different tokens can be forms of the same word.
  • representation for the unknown words
    Usually, we can represent only those words, which are present in the vocabulary. Giving information about subwords can help to represent out-of-vocabulary words relying of their spelling.
  • handing misspellings
    Even if on character in a word is wrong, this is another token, and, therefore, a completely different embedding (or even unknown word). With information about subwords, misspelled word would sill be the original one.

49

Tircks in Word2Vec

  1. word frequency
    Word2Vec uses a simple subsampling approach: each word $w_{i}$ in the training set is ignored with probability computed by fromula where $f(w_{i})$ is the word frequency and $thr$ is the chosen threshold (in the original paper $thr$ = $10^{-5}$)
    $P(w_{i})=1-\sqrt{\frac{thr}{f(w_{i})}}$

  2. distance from the central word
    we can assign higher weights to the words which are closer to the central.

context1 context2 context3 central context4 context5 context6
$\frac{1}{3}$ $\frac{2}{3}$ 1 0 1 $\frac{2}{3}$ $\frac{1}{3}$

48

Are all context words equally important for the Word2Vec?

  • word frequency
    The frequent words usually give less information than rare ones.
  • distance from the central word

47

In language, word order is important; specifically, left and right contexts have different meanings. How can we distinguish between the left and right contexts?
What we have to do is to evaluate co-occurences to the left and to the right separately.
HAL model

46

how to improve the simple co-occurrence counts?
words that are closer to the central are more important.
We can give closer words more weight.
HAL model

45

GloVe(Global Vectors for Word Representation)
the method uses global information form corpus to learn vectors.
$J(\theta)=\sum_{w,c} \in V f(N(w,c)) \cdot (u_{c}^{T}v_{w}+b_{c}+\bar{b_{w}}-logN(w,c))^2$
Glov controls the influence of rare and frequent words:

  • rare events are penalized,
  • very frequent events are not over-weighted.

the original GloVe paper

44

the gradient descent
$\theta^{new} = \theta^{old} - \alpha \nabla_{\theta} J(\theta)$
$\theta$ means parameters
$\alpha$ means learing rate $\alpha$
$\nabla$ means the gradient

43

softmax function
$softmax(x_{i})= \frac{exp(x_{i})}{\sum_{j=i}^{n}exp(x_{i})}$

  • max beacuse the largest $x_{i}$ will have the largest probability $p_{i}$
  • soft beacuse all probabilities $p_{i}$ are non-zero

42

Likelihood
$Likelihood=L(\theta)= \prod_{t=1}^{T} \prod_{-m \leq j \leq and j \neq 0}P(\omega_{t+j|\omega_{t},\theta})$
Negative Log-Likelihood(Loss)
$Loss = J(\theta) = - \frac{1}{T}logL(\theta) = - \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{-m \leq j \leq \ and \ j \neq 0}logP(\omega_{t+j|\omega_{t},\theta})$
go over text $\sum_{t=1}^{T}$
with a sliding window $\sum_{-m \leq j \leq m \ and \ j \neq 0}$
要满足概率大的函数编码小,所以取负
log 可以使乘法变为加法计算

41

Word2Vec

  • take a huge text corpus;
  • go over the text with a sliding window, moving one word at a time. At each step, there is a central word and context words(other words in this window);
  • for the central word, compute probabilities of context words;
  • adjust the vectors to increase these probabilities.
… I saw a cute grey cat playing in the gardeng …
$w_{t-2}$ $w_{t-1}$ $w_{t}$ $w_{t+1}$ $w_{t+1}$
context context central context context

40

Skip-gram(Continuous Skip-gram Model)
Skip-Gram is the model we considered so far: it predicts context words given the contral word. Skip-Gram with negative sampling is most popular approach.
用当前词来预测上下文
CBOW(Continuous Bag of Words Model)
CBOW predicts the centeral word from the sum of context vectors. This simaple sum of word vectors is called “bag of words”, which gives the name for the model.
通过上下文来预测当前值

39

打开.ipynb文件

38

Word2Vec

37

Latent Semantic Analysis(LSA)
$tf_idf(w,d,D) = tf(w,d) \cdot idf(w,D)$
dcoument d(from a collection D)

36

Positive Pointwise Mutual Information(PPMI)
$PPMI(w,c) = max(0,PMI(w,c))$
$PMI(w,c) = log \frac{p(w,c)}{p(w)p(c)} = log \frac{N(w,c)|(w,c)|}{N(w)N(c)}$

35

N(w,c) - number of times word w appears in context c

34

state-of-the-art
最先进的

33

Truncated Singular Value Decomposition(SVD) is function to reduce dimensionality.
奇异值分解，对角化，将特征值排序

32

distributional hypothesis
words which frequently appear in similar contexts have similar meaning.

31

nnlm
n-grams
rnn
cnn
lstm gru bilstm
seq2seq
attention

30

nlp course

29

torch.nn.Embedding
固定字典的大小，相当于tuple

28

论文一般更新forward() 算法
要熟悉激活函数是特性

27

In high dimensions, it is crucial to distribute probabilitymass where it matters rather than uniformly in all directions around each training point.

26

nlp-tutorial

25

Natural Language Processing with PyTorch

24

熵,信息熵,交叉熵
熵,在生物学中表示生物多样性的指标
信息熵
如果我们想存储一些数据(哈夫曼编码)，概率大的数，哈夫曼编码小
存储函数$S_{n}=\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{i}F(P_{i})$
要满足概率大的数，哈夫曼编码小
$F(p) = \frac{1}{p}$
信息熵公式
$H(P) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{i}logP_{i}$
信心熵取对数的原因

  • 取对数可以让原本不符合正态分部的模型符合正态分布，比如随着自变量的增加，因变量的方差也增大的模型取对数后会更加稳定
  • 取对数可以rescale,对数把乘法变成了加法

23

损失函数

  • 均方误差损失(mean squared error, MSE)
    $L_{MSE}(y,\hat{y}) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y-\hat{y})^2$
  • 分类交叉熵损失
    $L_{corss_entropy}(y,\hat{y})=-\sum_{i}y_{i}log(\hat{y_{i}})$

22

激活函数

  • Sigmoid
    $f(x)=\frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$
    sigmoid 函数将任意实数为输入并将其压入[0,1]。
    sigmoid 函数很快就趋近于饱和和接近最大值。因为它可能导致计算梯度为零或者溢出，这些现象称为梯度消失和梯度爆炸问题。
  • Tanh
    $f(x)=\frac{e^x-e^{-x}}{e^x+e^{-x}}$
    tanh和sigmoid函数都是一个挤压函数，tanh函数是将$[-\infty,+\infty]$压入$[-1,1]$
  • ReLU
    $f(x)=max(0,x)$
    ReLU代表整线性单元,ReLU函数所做的就是将负值映射为零。
  • Softmax
    $f(x)=\frac{e^{x_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{k}e^{x_j}}$
    softmax 函数将每个输出除以所有输出的和，所以概率分布和为1

21

n元语法指文本中连续出现n个词语。n元语法模型是基于阶马尔科夫链的一种概率语言模型，通过n个词出现的概率来推断语句的结构。
softmax 函数将每个输出除以所有输出的和，所以概率分布和为1

20

创建一个大小为(3,1)的随机张量，水平扩展4个副本

19

创建一个具有正态分布(mean=0,std=1)值张量

18

在区间[3,7]中创建一个形状为5x3的随机张量

17

向前求prediction，向后求梯度gred

16

PyTorch的基本操作

  • 创建张量
  • 操作常量
  • 对张量进行索引、切片、联合操作
  • 对张量进行梯度计算
  • CUDA框架下使用GPU操作张量

15

静态图要求首先声明、编译和执行计算图
动态图无需在每次执行之前都对模型进行编译

14

one-hot更常用

13

逆向文件频率(Inverse-Document-Frequency,IDF)
那么一个单词w的IDF(w)则表示为：
$IDF(w)= log\frac{N}{n_w}$
$n_w$ 表示存在单词w文本数量
N 表示所有文档总数
一些出现少但重要的词

12

向前传播主要是评估当前参数的损失，向后传播主要是根据损失函数更新参数

11

torch.transpose() 转置

output

10

torch.saves() 保存 torch.load() 加载

9

torch.squeeze() 压缩指定位置1维 torch.unsqueeze() 扩充指定位置1维

output

output

7

output

6

output

5

output

4

torch.cat() 拼接

output

3

张量类型 说明
torch.eye() 主对角线为1的二维张量
torch.linspace() 一维均匀分布张量 [start,end)
torch.logspace() 一维均匀分布张量，[$10^{start}$,$10^{end}$)
torch.rand() 均匀分布张量，[0,1)
torch.randn() 标准正态分布(均值0，方差1)从抽取随机数
torch.randperm() 0 到 n-1的整数随机排列
torch.arange() 包含start到end,以step为步长的组序
torch.range() 类似range()
torch.zeros() 返回一个全为标量0的张量

2

张量、矩阵、向量、标量
0维的张量就是标量，1维的张量就是向量，2维的张量就是矩阵，大于3维的张量没有名称，统一叫做张量。
标量：1,2,3
向量：[1,2,3],[1,2,4]
矩阵: [[1,2],[3,4]]
张量: [[[1,2],[3,4]],[[5,6],[7,8]]]

entry.title: prml
entry.content:
1 绪论

1.1 例子:多项式曲线拟合

概率论、决策论、信息论
概率论提供一个框架，用来以精确的数学形式描述这种不确定性
决策论让我们能够根据合适的标准，利用这种概率的表示，并进行最优预测

经常用来控制过拟合现象的一种技术是正则化(regulariztion)。这种技术涉及到给误差函数增加一个惩罚项，使得系数不会达到很大的值。

1.2.3 贝叶斯概率

自然语言表达贝叶斯定理
$posterior \propto likelihood \times prior$

先验贝叶斯定理
$p(w|D) = \frac{p(D|w)p(w)}{p(D)}$
D观测数据
w数量(例如多项式曲线拟合例子中的参数w)

频率学家广泛使用的一个估计是最大似然(maximum likehood)估计，其中w的值是使似然函数$p(D|w)$达到最大的w值。这对应于选择使观察到的数据集出现概率最大的w值。在机器学习的文献中，似然函数的负对数被叫做误差函数(error function)。由于负对数是单调递减的函数，最大化似然函数等价于最小化误差函数。

entry.title: research
entry.content:
3

Learning Spatiotemporal Features with 3D Convolutional Networks

Abstract

Our findings are three-fold:

  1. 3D ConvNets are more suitable for spatiotemporal feature learning

1

What Does BERT Look At? An Analysis of BERT’s Attention

Abstract

Complementary to there works, we propose methods for analyzing the attention mechanisms of pre-trained models and apply them to BERT.

1 Introduction

The strong results suggest pre-training teaches the models about the structure of language.

A surpresingly large amount of BERT’s attention focuses on the deliminator token [SEP], which we argue is used by the model as a sort of no-op.

Generally we find that attention heads in the same layer tend to behave similarly.

While on single head performs well at many relations, we find that particular heads correspond remarkably well to particular relations.

Our work suggests that to an extent this kind of syntax-aware attention already exist in BERT, which may be one of the reason for its success.

2 Background: Transformers and BERT

In the “masked language modeling” task, the model predicts the identites of words that have been masked-out of the input text.

In the “next sentence prediction” task, the model predicts whether the second half of the input follows the first half of the input in the corpus, or is a random paragraph.

We use <layer>-<head_number> to denote a particular attention head.

3 Surface-Level Patterns in Attention

3.1 Relative Position

We find that most heads put little attention on the current token. However, there are heads that specilize to attenting heavily on the next or previous token, especially in earlier layers of networ.

3.2 Attending to Separator Tokens

A similar pattern occurs for the uncased BERT model, suggesting there is a systematic reason for the attention to special tokens rather than it being an artifact of stochastic training.

One possible explanation is that [SEP] is used to aggregate segment-level information which can then be read by other heads.

Therefore, we speculate that attention over there special tokens might be used as a sort of “no-op” when the attention head’s function is applicable.

Starting in layer 5 - the same layer where attention to [SEP] becomes high - the gradients for attention to [SEP] become very small. This indicates that attending more or less to [SEP] does not substantially change BERT’s outputs, supporting the theory that attention to [SEP] is used as a no-op for attention heads.

3.3 Focused vs Board Attention

We find that some attention heads, especially in lower layers, have very broad attention.

This finding makes sense given that the representation for the [CLS] token is used as input for the “next sentence prediction” task during pre-training, so it attends boardly to aggregate a representation for the whole input in the last layer.

4 Probing Invididual Attention Heads

4.1 Method

We wish to evaluate attention heads at word-level tasks, but BERT uses byte-pair tokenization, which means some word are split up into mulitple tokens. We therefore convert token-token attention maps to word-word attention maps.

4.2 Dependency Syntax

Therefore as a point of comparison, we show predictions from a simple fixed-offset, baseline.

We also note heads can disagree with standard annotation conventions while still performing syntactic behavior.

While the similarity between machine-learned attention weights and human-defined syntactic relations are striking. we note there are relations for which attention heads do particularly well on.

We think it would be interesting future work to extend our analysis to see if the relations well-captured by attention are similar or different for other languages.

4.3 Coreference Resolution

Coreference links are usually longer than syntactic dependencies and state-of-the-art systems generally perform much worse at confernece compared to parsing.

It is particularly good with nominal mentions, perhaps because it is capable of fuzzy matching between synonyms.

5 Probing Attention Head Combinations

Attention-Only Probe. Our first probe learns a simple linear combination of attention weights.
$$ p(i|j) \propto exp (\sum_{k=1}^{n}w_{k}\alpha_{ij}^{k}+u_{k}\alpha_{ji}^{k}) $$
where $p(i|j)$ is the probability of word i being word j’s syntactic head, $\alpha_{ij}^{k}$ is the attention weight from word i to word j producted by head k, and n is the number of attention heads. We include both directions of attention: candidate head to dependent as well as dependent to candidate head. The weight vectors w and u are trained using standard supervised learning on the train set.

Attention-and-Words Probe.

6 Cluster Attention Heads

7 Related Work

8 Conclusion

entry.title: BSN: Boundary Sensitive Network for Temporal Action Proposal Generation
entry.content:
BSN: Boundary Sensitive Network for Temporal Action Proposal Generation

Abstract

Temporal action proposal generation is an important yet challenging problem, since temporal proposals with rich action content are indispensable for analysising real-world videos with long duration and high proportion irrelevant content.
This problem requires methods not only generating proposals with precise temporal boundaries, but also retrieving proposals to cover truth action instances with high recall and high overlap using relatively fewer proposals.
To address these difficulties, we introduce an effective proposal generation method, named Boundary-Sensitive Network (BSN), which adopts “local to global” fashion.
Locally, BSN first locates temporal boundaries with high probabilities, then directly combines these boundaries as proposals.
Globally, with Boundary-Sensitive Proposal feature, BSN retrieves proposals by evaluating the confidence of whether a proposal contains an action within its regions.

ActivityNet-1.3
THUMOS14

1 Introduction

However, videos in real scenarios are usually long, untrimmed and contain multiple action instances along with irrelavant contents.

Similar with object detection in spatial domain, temporal action detection task can be divided into two stages: proposal and classification.
Proposal generation stage aims to generate temporal video regions which may contain action instances, and classification stage aims to classifiy classes of candidate proposals.

Although classification methods have reached convincing performance, the detection precision is sitll low in many benchmarks.
Thus recently temporal action proposal generation has received much attention [3,4,5,6], aiming to improve the detection performance by improving the quality of proposals.

High quality proposals should come up with two key properites:

  1. proposals can cover truth action regions with both high recall and high temporal overlap.
  2. proposals are retrieved so that high recall and high overlap can be achieved using fewer proposals to reduce the computation cost of succeeding steps.

Most recently prosposal generation methods [3,4,5,7] generate proposals retrieving, while there is also method [6] making external boundaries regression.

To address these issues and generate high quality proposals, we propose the Boundary-Sensitive Network (BSN), which adopts “local to global” fashion to locally combine high probability boundaries as proposals and globally retrieve candidate proposals using proposal-level feature as shown in Fig 1.

In detail, BSN generates proposals in three steps.

  1. BSN evaluates the probabilities of each temporal location in video whether it is inside or outside, at or not at the boundaries of ground truth action instances, to generate starting, ending and actionness probabilites sequences as local information.
  2. BSN generates proposals via directly combining temporal locations with high starting and ending probabilites separately.
    Using this bottom-up fashion, BSN can generate proposals with flexible durations and precise boundaries.
  3. Finally, using features composed by actionness scores within and around proposal, BSN retrieves proposals by evaluating the confidence of whether a proposal contains an action.
    There proposal-level features offer global information for better evaluation.

In summary, the main contributions of our work are three-folds:

  1. We introduce a new architecture (BSN) base on “local to global” fashion to generate high quality temporal action proposals, which locally locates high boundary probability locations to achieve precise proposal boundaries and globally evaluates proposal-level feature to achieve reliable proposal confidence scores for retrieving.
  2. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves significantly better proposal quality than other state-of-the-art proposal generation methods, and can generate proposals in unseen action classes with comparative quality.
  3. Integrating our method with existing action classifier into detection framework leads to significantly improved performance on temporal action detection task.

2 Related work

Action recognition.

iDT HOF HOG MBH

Typically, two-stream network learns appearance and motion features based on RGB frame and optical flow field separately.
C3D network adopts 3D convolutional layers to directly capture both appearance and motion features from raw frames volume.

Object detection and proposals.

R-CNN[14] and its variations[15,16] construct an important branch of object detection methods, which adopt “detection by classifying proposals” framework.

proposals generation: sliding windows, HOG, Canny edge

Boundary probabilites are also adopted in LocNet[22] for revising the horizontal and vertical boundaries of existing proposals.
Our method differs in

  1. BSN aims to generate while LocNet aims to revise proposal.
  2. boundary probabilites are calculated repeatedly for all boxes in LocNet but only once for a video in BSN.

Temporal action detection and proposals

In our work, BSN can generate proposals with flexible boundaries meanwhile reliable confidence scores for retrieving.

3 Our Approach

3.1 Problem Definition

frames

Annotation of video X is composed by a set of action instances

where is the number of truth action instances in video X,
and are starting and ending time of action instance .

Unlike detection task, classes of action instances are not considered in temporal action proposal generatin.
Annotation set is used during training.
During prediction, generated proposals set should cover with high recall and temporal overlap.

3.2 Video Features Encoding

Two-stream network contains two branches:
spatial network operates on single RGB frame to capture appearance feature, and temporal network operates on stacked optical flow field to capture motion information.

  • a snippets sequence

is the length of snippet sequence.

  • a snippet

is the -th RGB frame in X

is stacked optical flow field derived around center frame

a regular frame interval and

  • encoded feature vector

output scores from spatial

output scores from temporal

  • a feature sequence

we use Soft-NMS algorithm to suppress redundant proposals by decaying their scores.

3.3 Boundary-Sensitive Network

Network architecture.

The architecture of BSN is presented in Fig 2(b), which contains three modules: temporal evaluation, proposal generation and proposal evaluation.

Temporal evaluation is a three layers temporal convolutional neural network.

Proposal generation constructs Boundary-Sensitive Proposal feature for each candidate proposal.

proposal evaluation is a multilayer perceptron model.

Temporal evaluation module.

tempoarl convolutional

For convenience of computation, we divide feature sequence into non-overlapped windows as the input of temporal evalutation module.

Proposal generation module.

The goal of proposal generation module is to generate candidate proposals and construct corresponding proposal-level feature.
We achieve this goal in two steps.

  1. we locate temporal locations with high boundary probabilites, and combine these locations to form proposals.
  2. for each proposal, we construct Boundary-Sensitive Proposal feature.

record the high starting probabilities and ending probabilities.

Any temporal region satisfying

and are minimum and maximum durations of ground truth action instances in dataset.

center region

starting region

ending region

are smapled with 16 linear interpolation.

are sampled the actionness sequence with 8 linear interpolation points

Boundary-Sensitive Proposal feature

a proposal

Proposal evaluation modules.

mlp

a generated proposal

are starting and ending probabilites in and separately.

3.4 Training of BSN

In BSN, temporal evaluation module is trained to learn local boundary and actionness probabilites from video features simulatanously. Then based on probabilites sequence generated by trained temporal evaluation module, we can generate proposals and corresponding BSP features and train the proposal evaluation module to learn the confidence score of proposals.

Temporal evalutation module.

The overall loss function consists of actionness loss, starting loss and ending loss:

, we adopt the sum of binary logistic regression loss fuction for all three tasks.

IoP is defined as the overlap ratio with groundturth proportional to the duration of this proposal.

, which are use for balancing the effect of positive and negative samples during training.

Proposal evaluation module.

simple regression loss

3.5 Prediction and Post-processing

To get final proposals set, we need to make score fusion to get final confidence score, the suppress redundant proposals based on these score.

Redundant proposals supression.

Soft-NMS[33]

4 Experiments

4.1 Dataset and setup

Dataset.

ActivityNet-1.3[1]

THUMOS14[2]

Evaluation metrics.

In temporal action proposal generation task:

Average Recall (AR)

IoU thresholds

Average Number of proposal (AN) = AR@AN

In temporal action detection task:

mean Average Precision (mAP)

IoU thresholds

Implementation detials.

For visual feature encoding, we use the two-stream network[11] with architecture described in [35], where BN-Inception network[36] is used as temporal network and ResNet network[37] is used as spatial network.

4.2 Temporal Proposal Generation

Table1: Comparision between our method with other state-of-the-art proposal generation methods on validation set of ActivityNet-1.3 in terms of AR@AN and AUC.

Table2: Comparision between our method with other state-of-the-art proposal generation methods on THUMOS14 in terms of AR@AN.

Table3: Generalization evaluation of BSN on ActivityNet-1.3. Seen subset: “Sports, Exercise, and Recreation”; Unseen subset: “Socializing, Relaxing, and Leisure”.

Table4: Study of effectivness of modules in BSN and contribution of components in BSP feature on THUMOS14, where PEM is trained with BSP feature constructed by Boundary region and Center region independently and jointly.

boudary regions contributes less improvements then center region.

4.3 Action Detection with Our Proposals

Table 5: Action detection results on validation and testing set of ActivityNet-1.3 in terms of mAP@IoU and average mAP, where our proposals are combine with video level classification results generated by [46].

Table 6: Action detection results on testing set of THUMOS14 in terms of mAP@IoU, where classification results generated by UntrimmedNet [48] and SCNN-classifier[7] are combined with proposals generated by BSN and other methods.

5 Conclusion

Our method can generate proposals with flexiable durations and precis boundaries via directly combing locations with high boundary probabilites, and make accurate retrieving via evaluating proposal confidence score with proposal-level features.

entry.title: Non-local Neural Networks
entry.content:
Non-local Neural Networks

Abstract

In this paper, we present non-local operations as a generic family of building blocks for capturing long-range dependencies.
Inspired by the classical non-local means method [4] in computer vision, our non-local operation computes the reponse at a postion as a weighted sum of the features at all positions.

This building block can be plugged into many computer vision architectures.

1. Introduction

Repeating local operation has several limitations.
First, it is computationally inefficient.
Second, it causes optimization difficulties that need to be carefully addressed [23, 21].
Finally, these challenges make multihop dependency modeling, e.g., when messages need to be delivered back and forth between distant positions, difficult.

Our proposed non-local operation is generation of the classical non-local mean operation [4] in computer vision.

Intuitively, a non-local operation computes the response at position as weighted sum of the feature at all positions in the input feature maps (Figure 1).

Figure 1. A position ’s response is computed by the weighted average of the features of all positions .

There are several advantages of using non-local operation:

  1. In constrast to the progressive behavior of recurrent and convolutional operations, non-local operation capture long-range dependencies directly by computing interactions between any two positions, regardless of their positional distence;
  2. As we show in experiments, non-local operations maintain the variable input size and can be easily combined with other operation.

To demonstrate the generality of non-local operations, we further present object detection/segmentation and pose estimation experiments on the COCO dataset[33].

Together with the evidence on videos, these images experiments show that non-local operations are generally useful and can become a basic building block in designing deep neural networks.

2. Related Work
